text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old female with stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant. Previously treated with dabrafenib/trametinib with initial partial response followed by progressive disease at approximately 8 months. Subsequently initiated combination ipilimumab/nivolumab immunotherapy; treatment course was complicated by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis after cycle 2, requiring hospitalization, high-dose IV corticosteroids, and a single dose of infliximab. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued. Following recovery and steroid taper completion, patient was restarted on nivolumab monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance immunotherapy. This examination is obtained approximately 12 weeks after resumption of single-agent nivolumab (approximately 5 months after the first dose of combination immunotherapy) for restaging purposes. Comparison made to prior CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed at time of disease progression on dabrafenib/trametinib.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of iohexol intravenous contrast material during the portal venous phase. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformatted images were reviewed on a diagnostic workstation. Slice thickness 2.5 mm. No oral contrast administered.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules or masses are identified. No areas of consolidation or ground-glass opacity. No pleural effusions. The visualized airways are patent and unremarkable. No endobronchial lesions.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is not widened. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thymus is not enlarged. No pericardial effusion. The aorta is of normal caliber throughout its thoracic course. No evidence of vascular abnormality.

Axilla: The previously enlarged and necrotic left axillary lymph node has undergone substantial interval reduction in size. It now measures approximately 1.3 x 0.9 cm in the axial plane (previously 3.4 x 2.8 cm at time of progression on targeted therapy). The central necrosis previously noted is no longer appreciable. There remains a small residual soft tissue density at this site consistent with a lymph node undergoing treatment response, though it falls short of normal size criteria. The right axilla is unremarkable. No new axillary adenopathy identified bilaterally.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesions. No intrathoracic soft tissue masses.

Bones (thoracic): No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. No pathologic fractures within the imaged portion of the thoracic skeleton.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. There has been marked interval reduction in the hepatic tumor burden compared to the prior examination at progression. The previously dominant lesion in hepatic segment VI has substantially decreased in size and now appears as a faint hypodense focus measuring approximately 0.9 x 0.7 cm (previously 2.6 x 2.1 cm). Enhancement characteristics are diminished compared to the prior examination, suggesting treatment effect. The previously identified segment II lesion, which measured 1.5 cm at progression, has markedly regressed and is now barely perceptible as a small hypodense focus measuring approximately 0.6 cm, without appreciable mass effect. The segment IVa lesion, which measured 1.2 cm at progression, is no longer identified on this examination. No new hepatic lesions are detected. There is no biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent. No perihepatic ascites.

Gallbladder and biliary system: The gallbladder is normally distended without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated (3 mm).

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size (10.3 cm in length) and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in size, morphology, and enhancement throughout the head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic fluid collections or masses.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys and ureters: Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No perinephric fluid. The ureters are not dilated.

Gastrointestinal tract: The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness without evidence of bowel wall thickening or pneumatosis. Note is made of mild mucosal enhancement in the left colon and sigmoid, which in the clinical context of recent immune-mediated colitis likely reflects residual post-inflammatory change; however, this is substantially improved compared to expected appearance at the time of acute colitis, and no frank bowel wall thickening is identified. No free intraperitoneal air.

Pelvis and retroperitoneum: No pelvic masses or free fluid. The uterus and adnexa appear unremarkable. The urinary bladder is normally distended. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. The right inguinal lymph node, which measured 1.2 cm at the time of progression on dabrafenib/trametinib, has decreased to approximately 0.5 cm and no longer meets criteria for pathological enlargement. No left inguinal adenopathy.

Retroperitoneal lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy identified.

Subcutaneous and soft tissue: The previously identified subcutaneous nodule along the left flank has markedly diminished. There is a faint, barely perceptible residual area of soft tissue thickening in the left flank subcutaneous fat measuring approximately 0.5 cm (previously 1.4 cm at progression). This is approaching the limit of CT resolution. No new subcutaneous nodules or cutaneous masses are identified over the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

Aorta and great vessels (abdominal): The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. No aneurysmal dilatation. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and renal arteries are patent.

Bones (abdominal and pelvis): No lytic or blastic osseous lesions in the visualized lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvic bones. No pathologic fractures.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked and favorable interval change in the known sites of metastatic melanoma since the prior examination performed at disease progression on dabrafenib/trametinib, consistent with a substantial treatment response to immunotherapy.

2. Left axillary lymph node: dramatically reduced in size from 3.4 cm to approximately 1.3 cm, with resolution of previously noted central necrosis. Small residual soft tissue density remains.

3. Hepatic disease: impressive regression of all hepatic metastases. The dominant segment VI lesion has shrunk from 2.6 cm to approximately 0.9 cm. The segment II lesion has collapsed from 1.5 cm to a barely perceptible 0.6 cm focus. The segment IVa lesion is no longer identifiable.

4. Right inguinal lymph node: decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.5 cm, now within normal size limits.

5. Left flank subcutaneous nodule: markedly regressed, now only faintly perceptible at approximately 0.5 cm.

6. No new sites of metastatic disease identified anywhere in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

7. Mild residual colonic mucosal changes in the left colon and sigmoid, in keeping with the clinical history of recent immune-mediated colitis, now substantially improved.

8. No intrathoracic adenopathy or pulmonary metastases.

Overall, the findings represent a robust and clinically meaningful response to checkpoint immunotherapy across all previously documented disease sites. Residual foci in the left axilla, liver, and left flank are substantially diminished relative to the nadir of disease at progression.
